description:
You are acting as the company’s release-manager bot. Your goal is to ship version 1.0.0 of our internal web application, bring the service online, and hand back proof that the deployment is live. The project lives in the normal user’s home directory: /home/user All work MUST stay inside the following single directory tree so that the CI runner has predictable places to look: /home/user/releases • Do NOT create files anywhere else. • The agent will **not** receive root privileges. ----------------------------------------------------------------------- STEP-BY-STEP OBJECTIVES ----------------------------------------------------------------------- 1. Build the release folder Create the directory /home/user/releases/release-1.0.0 and put the static web assets for the new release inside it. 2. Provide the homepage Inside the release directory, write a file named “index.html”. Its **entire** byte-for-byte content must be EXACTLY as shown below (including blank lines and indentation): Release 1.0.0

Application: Acme WebApp

Release version: 1.0.0

Status: Live

3. Launch the web-server • Serve the content on TCP port 8000 on the loopback interface. • It must stay running in the background after your terminal returns. • Any standard module available in a stock Python 3 installation is allowed (e.g. http.server). • Redirect the server’s STDOUT and STDERR to a file called /home/user/releases/server.log 4. Add a human-readable start-up marker Immediately before starting the server, append a single line to “server.log” **in this exact format** (the timestamp values must be the current time when the write happens): YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - Starting server on port 8000 Example: 2023-08-15 14:07:03 - Starting server on port 8000 (The automated test will ignore the actual numbers but will look for the pattern shown above.) 5. Verify the deployment from the same machine Using any shell utility (curl is installed), request the root document from the server: http://localhost:8000/ Capture the result of that single request in a new file named /home/user/releases/deployment_verification.log The verification file must contain **only three lines** in exactly this order: LINE 1 → The HTTP status line returned by the server (e.g. “HTTP/1.0 200 OK”). LINE 2 → The length in bytes of the response body, as a plain integer (no extra text). LINE 3 → The literal string “VERIFIED”. ----------------------------------------------------------------------- WHAT THE AUTOMATED GRADER WILL CHECK ----------------------------------------------------------------------- 1. Directory /home/user/releases/release-1.0.0 exists. 2. /home/user/releases/release-1.0.0/index.html exists and its SHA-256 hash matches the exact file block shown above. 3. A process owned by the user is listening on 127.0.0.1:8000 and its command line matches “python* http.server*”. 4. /home/user/releases/server.log exists and contains at least one line matching the regular expression ^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2} - Starting server on port 8000$ 5. /home/user/releases/deployment_verification.log exists and • has **exactly** three newline-terminated lines • LINE 1 starts with “HTTP/” and ends with “200 OK” • LINE 2 is an integer that equals the byte length of index.html • LINE 3 is exactly “VERIFIED”. ----------------------------------------------------------------------- IMPORTANT IMPLEMENTATION NOTES ----------------------------------------------------------------------- • Most shells will detach a Python web-server by appending “&” or by using other job-control constructs; choose any method that does not require sudo. • For maximum portability, prefer IPv4 loopback (127.0.0.1). • Make sure the verification curl actually hits the running server and not a stale cache; always request “http://localhost:8000/”. • The grader will wait up to 15 seconds for port 8000 to become reachable; do not spawn the server in a subshell that terminates prematurely. ----------------------------------------------------------------------- DELIVERABLES SUMMARY ----------------------------------------------------------------------- When you are done, the filesystem and running processes must look exactly like this: /home/user/releases/ ├── release-1.0.0/ │ └── index.html (file, contents exactly as specified) ├── server.log (contains the single-line timestamp marker │ plus any server output generated later) └── deployment_verification.log (3-line proof of successful fetch) One python3 http.server process must be listening on 127.0.0.1:8000. The grader performs no manual reading; every check is automated and insensitive to terminal colour codes. Success on all five bullet points in the “WHAT THE AUTOMATED GRADER WILL CHECK” list means the task is complete.
truth:
1. Expected directory path: /home/user/releases/release-1.0.0 2. Expected index.html exact byte sequence (in UTF-8 without BOM): Release 1.0.0

Application: Acme WebApp

Release version: 1.0.0

Status: Live

SHA-256 of the above (including trailing newline) = 6b835e2cc1b476a07cdaa8e44e567afc794484514797f5ad1baf583c8e5dbd55 3. server.log must exist at /home/user/releases/server.log Must contain at least one line matching regex: ^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2} - Starting server on port 8000$ 4. The web-server process must satisfy: a) Executable: /usr/bin/python3 (path may vary inside container) b) Argv must include “-m”, “http.server”, and “8000” c) Must be bound to 127.0.0.1:8000 (IPv4) 5. deployment_verification.log path: /home/user/releases/deployment_verification.log • Exactly 3 lines, each ending with \n • Line 1 regex: ^HTTP/.* 200 OK$ • Line 2 must equal len(index.html) in bytes = 203 • Line 3 must equal “VERIFIED” 6. File and directory permissions: default umask OK (no chmod needed). Process must run under UID “user”. Any deviation causes the grader to mark the task as failed.
name: task_000000_da7b6d1a
